network.overall_strategy: The network centers on the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic homologation pathway—the only detailed mechanistic proposal for prebiotic arginine synthesis—and extends it with a credible ornithine-based guanidinylation branch derived from internal network intermediates. HCN is generated via two routes: (1) hydrothermal formamide thermal decomposition and (2) atmospheric electric discharge. Cyanamide is generated via two routes: (1) urea dehydration on clay and (2) HCN UV photochemical isomerization. These four source variants feed into the core cyanosulfidic sequence (acrylonitrile → β-aminopropionitrile → β-aminopropionaldehyde → guanidinium hemiaminal → compound 46 → arginine). Additionally, β-aminopropionaldehyde branches into a Strecker route to ornithine, which undergoes cyanamide-mediated guanidinylation to give arginine. This produces 10 distinct pathways through combinatorial variation of HCN sources, cyanamide sources, and two final assembly strategies (compound 46 hydrolysis vs. ornithine guanidinylation).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, H2-rich fluid, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed on greigite surface (structural analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters) and reduced by H₂ via surface hydride transfer to produce formate. The reaction proceeds through a metal-bound carboxylate intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: CO₂ reduction to formate on Fe-S minerals is one of the best-established hydrothermal prebiotic reactions. Greigite catalyzes this reduction under alkaline vent conditions, analogous to the acetyl-CoA pathway in modern methanogens. This provides the first C1 building block for more complex molecules (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; reviewed in Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Formate amidation to formamide on FeS surfaces

conditions.other: NH₃-rich hydrothermal fluid, mineral surface-mediated condensation

reactions.mechanism: Ammonium formate undergoes thermal condensation on iron sulfide mineral surfaces. The mineral surface lowers the activation barrier for C–N bond formation, producing formamide with loss of water. This is a thermally driven amidation favored at elevated temperature and pressure.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal condensation of ammonium formate to formamide is well-established in hydrothermal settings. Saladino et al. (2012) showed formamide is a key prebiotic intermediate at hydrothermal temperatures. Iron sulfide mineral surfaces catalyze this condensation by stabilizing the transition state. Formamide serves as a crucial precursor to HCN.

reactions.name: Thermal decomposition of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: Thermal dehydration; occurs at mineral-water interfaces near vent outlets where temperatures are highest

reactions.mechanism: Formamide undergoes thermal dehydration: HCONH₂ → HCN + H₂O. This is a well-established unimolecular elimination reaction that proceeds via a four-center transition state. Mineral surfaces (particularly pyrite and metal oxides) lower the activation energy and provide adsorption sites that favor dehydration over hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decomposition of formamide to HCN is one of the most robust prebiotic HCN generation routes. Saladino et al. (2001, 2012) extensively documented this reaction on various mineral surfaces including iron oxides and sulfides at 400-500K. This avoids the chemically dubious formaldehyde + NH₃ → HCN route criticized in previous attempts. The reaction is strongly favored thermodynamically at elevated temperatures.

reactions.name: Atmospheric electric discharge production of HCN and NH₃

conditions.catalyst: electric discharge (lightning); mineral surfaces concentrate products
conditions.other: Reducing atmosphere (CH₄, NH₃, H₂O, H₂); HCN generated in atmosphere dissolves into surface pools containing mineral surfaces that concentrate cyanide

reactions.mechanism: Lightning/electric discharge in a reducing atmosphere containing CH₄ and NH₃ generates HCN through radical recombination of CH and N fragments. HCN (along with other products) dissolves into surface pools where mineral surfaces adsorb and concentrate it. Montmorillonite adsorbs HCN and related nitriles, enabling accumulation.
reactions.reasoning: Miller-Urey experiments (Miller 1953, 1986) established that electric discharge in reducing atmospheres produces HCN as a major product. Miller (1986) explicitly identified HCN as a central prebiotic feedstock molecule. Mineral surfaces in receiving pools concentrate the dissolved HCN. This provides an independent, well-established HCN source distinct from the hydrothermal formamide route.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal urea synthesis from CO₂ and NH₃

conditions.other: High pressure required to drive condensation; NH₃-rich hydrothermal fluids; proceeds via ammonium carbamate intermediate

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ reacts with excess NH₃ to form ammonium carbamate (NH₄⁺·NH₂COO⁻), which undergoes thermally driven dehydration at high pressure to yield urea: CO₂ + 2NH₃ → NH₂CONH₂ + H₂O. Mineral surfaces stabilize the carbamate intermediate and promote water loss.
reactions.reasoning: Urea synthesis from CO₂ and ammonia under hydrothermal conditions is industrially well-known and has prebiotic analogs. Ammonium carbamate forms readily at moderate temperatures and converts to urea at elevated temperature and pressure. Hydrothermal vents provide the necessary conditions. Urea has been identified as a plausible prebiotic molecule in multiple studies.

reactions.name: Urea dehydration to cyanamide on clay

conditions.other: Dry phase of wet-dry cycling; urea concentrated on clay surfaces undergoes dehydration during evaporation

reactions.mechanism: Urea adsorbed on montmorillonite clay surface undergoes thermal dehydration during the dry phase of wet-dry cycling: NH₂CONH₂ → NH₂CN + H₂O. The clay surface catalyzes this elimination by stabilizing the transition state and providing acid-base sites. Montmorillonite's interlayer environment promotes dehydration.
reactions.reasoning: Dehydration of urea to cyanamide on clay minerals under dry heating is well-established. This provides the primary cyanamide source for the cyanosulfidic network. Patel et al. (2015) identified cyanamide as a critical reagent for guanidine group installation. The wet-dry cycling environment naturally produces cyanamide from urea concentrated on clay surfaces.

reactions.name: HCN UV photochemical isomerization to cyanamide

conditions.other: UV-exposed surface pool; photoisomerization of HCN to HNC followed by dimerization; TiO₂ enhances photochemical yield

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation isomerizes HCN to HNC (hydrogen isocyanide). HNC then reacts with HCN through [2+1] cycloaddition or stepwise radical coupling to form carbodiimide (HN=C=NH), which tautomerizes to cyanamide (H₂N-CN). TiO₂ photocatalysis enhances the initial isomerization. This is a less efficient but independent route to cyanamide.
reactions.reasoning: UV photochemistry of HCN producing isocyanide and subsequent dimerization products is documented in astrochemical and prebiotic literature. While less efficient than the urea dehydration route (rxn_006), it provides an alternative cyanamide source that does not require hydrothermal urea. This adds network redundancy. The route is speculative relative to the urea pathway but supported by HCN photochemistry studies (Ferris & Hagan, 1984).

reactions.name: Cu(I)-catalyzed coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycle; H₂S as sacrificial reductant
conditions.other: UV irradiation (~300 nm); shallow photic surface pool; Cu minerals provide dissolved Cu(I); >25% yield reported

reactions.mechanism: UV light drives Cu(II)→Cu(I) photoreduction using H₂S as electron donor. Cu(I) activates acetylene toward nucleophilic addition of cyanide, producing acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH-CN) via a copper-acetylide intermediate. The Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycle is maintained by continuous UV irradiation and H₂S supply.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nat. Chem. 2015) demonstrated this reaction with >25% yield. Cu(I)-catalyzed hydrocyanation of acetylene under UV with H₂S as reductant is the key entry point to the cyanosulfidic arginine pathway. Copper sulfide minerals (e.g., covellite, chalcocite) provide the necessary Cu ions. This is one of the best-supported steps in the entire arginine network.

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile giving β-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: aqueous, mildly basic conditions; clay surfaces concentrate reactants
conditions.other: Neutral to slightly basic pH; spontaneous conjugate addition in aqueous ammonia

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia acts as a nucleophile in a 1,4-conjugate (Michael) addition to the electron-poor alkene of acrylonitrile. The reaction proceeds through: NH₃ attacks the β-carbon of CH₂=CH-CN, generating the enolate of the nitrile, which protonates to give NH₂-CH₂-CH₂-CN (β-aminopropionitrile). This is a facile, well-known reaction in aqueous solution.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identified this Michael addition as a key step in the arginine pathway. The conjugate addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile is a standard, high-yielding reaction that proceeds readily in aqueous solution at ambient conditions. β-Aminopropionitrile is the central hub intermediate from which both the compound 46 and ornithine branches diverge.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of β-aminopropionitrile to β-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation; H₂S as sacrificial reductant; Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox
conditions.other: Acidic conditions favor photoreduction; shallow UV-illuminated pool

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of β-aminopropionitrile is photoreduced by H₂S under UV irradiation through a copper-mediated photoredox cycle. H₂S donates electrons via Cu(II)→Cu(I) photoreduction, generating a thioimidate intermediate that hydrolyzes to the aldehyde β-aminopropionaldehyde (NH₂-CH₂-CH₂-CHO).
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) described UV-driven photoreduction of nitriles to aldehydes using H₂S as reductant in the cyanosulfidic network. This step converts β-aminopropionitrile to the corresponding aldehyde, which is the key branch point: it feeds into both the guanidinium hemiaminal (toward compound 46) and the Strecker route (toward ornithine). The photoreduction proceeds with good yield under the reported conditions.

reactions.name: Cyanamide addition to β-aminopropionaldehyde forming cyclic guanidinium hemiaminal

conditions.catalyst: aqueous, neutral to mildly acidic pH; clay surfaces concentrate reactants

reactions.mechanism: The primary amine of β-aminopropionaldehyde attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide (NH₂-C≡N) in a nucleophilic addition, forming a guanidine linkage. The aldehyde then cyclizes intramolecularly with the newly formed guanidine NH to produce a six-membered cyclic guanidinium hemiaminal. This installs the critical guanidino group of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identified cyanamide as the guanidinylating agent in the arginine pathway. The addition of cyanamide to the amine group, followed by intramolecular cyclization with the aldehyde, is described as proceeding with good yield. This is the key step that distinguishes the arginine pathway from other amino acid syntheses—it installs the guanidinium group that has proven so elusive in other prebiotic schemes (Miller 1986; Pol et al. 2022).

reactions.name: Kiliani-Fischer homologation and photoreduction to arginine α-aminonitrile (compound 46)

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation; H₂S as reductant; Cu photoredox; sequential HCN addition
conditions.other: Multi-step sequence: HCN addition (Kiliani-Fischer), photoreduction of intermediate nitrile/thioamide, cyclization through thiolactam, further HCN addition and reduction to give compound 46

reactions.mechanism: The cyclic guanidinium hemiaminal undergoes Kiliani-Fischer carbon chain elongation via HCN addition to the aldehyde/hemiaminal, producing a cyanohydrin. Subsequent UV/H₂S photoreduction converts nitrile intermediates through thioamide and thiolactam (γ-butyrothiolactam) stages. A final HCN addition generates an α-aminonitrile. The net result is chain elongation from C4 to C6 with installation of the α-amino and α-nitrile groups, producing compound 46 (2-amino-5-guanidinopentanenitrile).
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) described this multi-step homologation sequence in detail, with individual steps giving 'uniformly good to excellent' yields. The sequence proceeds through a thiolactam intermediate and involves iterative Kiliani-Fischer HCN addition and H₂S photoreduction. This is represented as a single reaction node because the intermediates between the cyclic hemiaminal and compound 46 are transient and not individually isolable under the one-pot photochemical conditions. This is the most complex step but is well-documented in the Patel et al. Supplementary Information.

reactions.name: Alkaline hydrolysis of arginine α-aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.catalyst: alkaline pH (9-11); no mineral catalyst required
conditions.other: Alkaline aqueous solution; may also proceed at neutral pH over longer timescales; could be facilitated by transport to alkaline pool or mixing with vent effluent

reactions.mechanism: The α-nitrile group of compound 46 undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to an amide (via nucleophilic addition of OH⁻ or H₂O), then to the carboxylic acid. The overall transformation is R-CN + 2H₂O → R-COOH + NH₃. Under alkaline conditions this hydrolysis is kinetically accessible at moderate temperatures, yielding free arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids under alkaline conditions is one of the most fundamental organic reactions and the final step in any Strecker-type amino acid synthesis. Patel et al. (2015) noted that alkaline hydrolysis of compound 46 would give arginine. This step could occur in alkaline hydrothermal vent effluent mixing with surface waters, or in evaporitic alkaline pools. The kinetic barrier is modest at pH 10-11 and 300-350K.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of ornithine α-aminonitrile from β-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: aqueous, neutral to mildly basic pH; clay surfaces concentrate reactants and catalyze imine formation
conditions.other: Classic Strecker conditions: aldehyde + NH₃ → imine, then + HCN → α-aminonitrile

reactions.mechanism: β-Aminopropionaldehyde (NH₂-CH₂-CH₂-CHO) reacts with NH₃ to form an imine (Schiff base) at the aldehyde position. HCN then attacks the imine carbon nucleophilically, producing the α-aminonitrile NH₂-CH₂-CH₂-CH(NH₂)-CN, which is ornithine α-aminonitrile. The δ-amino group is already present from the β-aminopropionitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the most well-established route to α-aminonitriles (Miller 1953, 1986). By using β-aminopropionaldehyde (already produced in rxn_010 from the cyanosulfidic network) as the aldehyde substrate, this reaction produces ornithine's α-aminonitrile precursor. This elegantly connects the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic network to the metabolic-analog (ornithine) pathway, providing a credible prebiotic ornithine source—addressing the critical gap identified in previous attempts where ornithine had no synthesis route.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of ornithine α-aminonitrile to ornithine

conditions.catalyst: mildly alkaline pH; no mineral catalyst required
conditions.other: Aqueous hydrolysis; may be accelerated by alkaline conditions or mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Standard nitrile hydrolysis: the α-CN group of ornithine aminonitrile is hydrolyzed via amide intermediate to the carboxylic acid, yielding ornithine (2,5-diaminopentanoic acid) and releasing NH₃.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is the final Strecker step, well-documented for prebiotic amino acid production (Miller 1953, 1986). This provides a complete prebiotic route to ornithine from network intermediates, avoiding the problematic multi-step glutamate→ornithine conversion (which requires enzymatic steps in biology and was criticized in Attempt 1). Ornithine itself has been identified as a non-proteinogenic amino acid relevant to prebiotic chemistry (Glavin et al. 2025).

reactions.name: Guanidinylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.catalyst: mildly acidic to neutral pH; possible mineral surface enhancement
conditions.other: Concentrated prebiotic pool; may be enhanced by wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: The δ-amino group of ornithine (NH₂-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂-CH(NH₂)-COOH) attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide (NH₂-C≡N) in a nucleophilic addition. Proton transfer and tautomerization yield the guanidino group: R-NH₂ + NH₂CN → R-NH-C(=NH)-NH₂. The product is arginine, with the characteristic guanidinium side chain.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is a well-known guanidinylating agent that transfers a C(=NH) unit to primary amines. Patel et al. (2015) identified cyanamide as the key guanidine source in their arginine pathway. Glavin et al. (2025) noted that ornithine could serve as a precursor to arginine via guanidinylation. While the specific ornithine + cyanamide → arginine reaction has not been demonstrated under prebiotic conditions, the underlying chemistry (amine + cyanamide → guanidine) is well-established in organic chemistry. This remains speculative but represents the most chemically plausible guanidinylation route (Pol et al. 2022 acknowledge this as an open question).

reactions.name: Serpentinization production of H₂ and NH₃

conditions.other: Serpentinization of ultramafic rock; Fe⁰ reduces water to H₂; nitrogen species reduced to NH₃

reactions.mechanism: Serpentinization of ultramafic rock (olivine + pyroxene) by seawater produces H₂ through oxidation of Fe²⁺/Fe⁰ in the mineral structure. Dissolved N₂ or NO₃⁻ is reduced to NH₃ by the strongly reducing conditions (Fe⁰ + H₂). The overall process: 2(Mg,Fe)₂SiO₄ + 3H₂O → Mg₃Si₂O₅(OH)₄ + Fe₃O₄ + H₂, and N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Serpentinization is the primary source of H₂ and NH₃ in alkaline hydrothermal vent systems (Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007). H₂ concentrations of 1-16 mM are measured at modern Lost City-type vents. NH₃ production from dissolved nitrogen under reducing conditions on Fe⁰/FeNi alloy surfaces has been demonstrated experimentally. This provides the reducing equivalents and nitrogen source for the entire network.

reactions.name: Selective adsorption and concentration of arginine on montmorillonite

conditions.other: Cation exchange; arginine's guanidinium group (pKa ~12.5) is permanently protonated under prebiotic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Arginine's positively charged guanidinium side chain undergoes cation exchange with interlayer cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺) of montmorillonite, resulting in 30-50× stronger binding compared to neutral amino acids. This selective adsorption concentrates arginine on clay surfaces, protecting it from dilution and degradation.
reactions.reasoning: A recent study found L-arginine and lysine bind montmorillonite ~30-50× more tightly than neutral amino acids via cation exchange (referenced in the research findings). This selective concentration mechanism is critical for arginine accumulation given its expected low yields from any prebiotic pathway. The guanidinium group's permanent positive charge at any prebiotic pH drives this selectivity. This is not a synthetic reaction but a crucial concentration/preservation step (Frenkel-Pinter et al. 2019-2020).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN + urea-derived cyanamide → cyanosulfidic compound 46 → arginine
pathways.description: Complete Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical pathway. HCN from hydrothermal formamide decomposition and cyanamide from urea dehydration on clay feed the full Patel et al. cyanosulfidic homologation sequence to compound 46, which hydrolyzes to arginine. This is the primary, best-supported route.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN + urea-derived cyanamide → cyanosulfidic compound 46 → arginine
pathways.description: Uses atmospheric electric discharge as HCN source instead of hydrothermal formamide. Cyanamide from hydrothermal urea dehydration on clay. Otherwise follows the same Patel et al. cyanosulfidic sequence. Provides environmental redundancy for HCN sourcing.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN + HCN-derived cyanamide → cyanosulfidic compound 46 → arginine
pathways.description: Both HCN and cyanamide sourced from hydrothermal formamide decomposition. Cyanamide produced by UV photochemical isomerization of HCN. Fully hydrothermal-sourced carbon and nitrogen. Less well-supported cyanamide route but provides network diversity.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN + HCN-derived cyanamide → cyanosulfidic compound 46 → arginine
pathways.description: Fully atmospheric/surface pathway: HCN from electric discharge and cyanamide from UV photochemistry of HCN. No hydrothermal input required. The most self-contained surface pathway, though the HCN→cyanamide photochemistry is the least established route.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN + urea-derived cyanamide → ornithine Strecker → guanidinylation → arginine
pathways.description: Diverges from the cyanosulfidic pathway at β-aminopropionaldehyde. Instead of forming the guanidinium hemiaminal, a Strecker synthesis on β-aminopropionaldehyde produces ornithine α-aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to ornithine, then reacts with cyanamide to give arginine. This provides a credible ornithine source from within the network.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN + urea-derived cyanamide → ornithine Strecker → guanidinylation → arginine
pathways.description: Same as P5 but with atmospheric HCN source. Atmospheric electric discharge provides HCN, which enters the cyanosulfidic sequence to generate β-aminopropionaldehyde, then branches into the ornithine/guanidinylation route.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN + HCN-photolytic cyanamide → ornithine Strecker → guanidinylation → arginine
pathways.description: Ornithine-based route where both HCN and cyanamide derive from the hydrothermal formamide→HCN source, with cyanamide formed by UV photolysis. Tests whether a purely HCN-derived system can generate arginine through the ornithine branch.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN + HCN-photolytic cyanamide → ornithine → guanidinylation → arginine
pathways.description: Fully atmospheric/surface ornithine pathway. HCN from electric discharge provides both the carbon backbone (via acrylonitrile) and the guanidine group (via photolytic cyanamide). Ornithine formed by Strecker chemistry on β-aminopropionaldehyde.

pathways.name: Dual HCN source convergence → cyanosulfidic compound 46 → arginine
pathways.description: Convergent pathway exploiting both hydrothermal and atmospheric HCN sources simultaneously feeding into acrylonitrile synthesis. Dual sourcing increases effective HCN concentration in surface pools, improving acrylonitrile yield. Cyanamide from urea route. Demonstrates environmental cooperation between hydrothermal and atmospheric HCN delivery.

pathways.name: Full convergent network: dual HCN + dual cyanamide → bifurcating compound 46 + ornithine → arginine
pathways.description: Maximum convergence pathway utilizing all four entry points (2 HCN sources × 2 cyanamide sources) and both final routes to arginine (compound 46 hydrolysis and ornithine guanidinylation). β-Aminopropionaldehyde serves as the critical branch point feeding both routes. This represents the full network operating simultaneously, maximizing arginine yield through redundant and parallel pathways.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway faithfully reproduces the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic route with hydrothermal HCN via formamide decomposition and urea-derived cyanamide. The hydrothermal segment (CO₂ → formate → formamide → HCN) is well-supported though rxn_002 (formate amidation) has limited experimental precedent. The surface cyanosulfidic sequence is directly from the literature. Urea dehydration on clay to cyanamide is plausible under drying conditions. The final nitrile hydrolysis to arginine is standard chemistry. Longest path at 12 steps, but each is justified.
suggestions: Provide more detail on formate-to-formamide mechanism on FeS; consider whether ammonium formate thermal conversion is a better description
Serpentinization producing NH₃ directly from H₂O is oversimplified—should explicitly include N₂ or nitrate as nitrogen source

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shorter pathway using atmospheric HCN (electric discharge) and urea-derived cyanamide. Classic Miller-type HCN production is well-supported. The Surface environment label for atmospheric discharge is slightly imprecise but functionally correct since products dissolve into surface pools. Urea route to cyanamide is solid. The core cyanosulfidic sequence is the strongest part. Good overall coherence.
suggestions: Clarify that rxn_004 is atmospheric with products dissolving into surface ponds
Specify mineral catalyst for surface environment more explicitly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses hydrothermal HCN and HCN→cyanamide UV photoisomerization. The photoisomerization step (rxn_007) is the weakest link—while HCN can photoisomerize to HNC, the full conversion to cyanamide (H₂NCN) requires H-migration and is not well-established as a high-yield process. This adds uncertainty. Otherwise follows the strong cyanosulfidic core.
suggestions: Provide experimental evidence or mechanistic detail for HCN→cyanamide photoisomerization yield
Consider whether this step proceeds through HNC or another intermediate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shortest compound 46 route at 8 steps. Atmospheric HCN + HCN photoisomerization to cyanamide. Efficient but the cyanamide source (rxn_007) is the weakest step. Without the urea route for cyanamide, this pathway depends on questionable photoisomerization. The cyanosulfidic core remains strong.
suggestions: Strengthen evidence for HCN photoisomerization or add alternative cyanamide source

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Critical carbon count error: Strecker synthesis from β-aminopropionaldehyde (3C) + HCN yields a 4-carbon α-aminonitrile (2,4-diaminobutanenitrile, C₄H₉N₃), NOT ornithine α-aminonitrile (C₅H₁₁N₃). mol_018 is labeled C₄H₉N₃ which is consistent with the Strecker product, but this hydrolyzes to 2,4-diaminobutyric acid (C₄), not ornithine (C₅). The hydrolysis step (rxn_015) claims C₄H₉N₃ → C₅H₁₂N₂O₂ which violates carbon conservation. This fundamental error invalidates the ornithine branch.
suggestions: Correct the carbon count: use a 4-carbon aldehyde (e.g., 4-aminobutanal) as Strecker substrate for ornithine
Or replace ornithine branch entirely with verified chemistry
Consider glutamate → ornithine route mentioned in Glavin et al. (2025)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Same carbon count error as P5 in the ornithine branch. The early steps are sound but the Strecker → ornithine conversion is chemically incorrect. The guanidinylation of ornithine by cyanamide is also undemonstrated prebiotically per the literature.
suggestions: Fix carbon count in ornithine α-aminonitrile
Provide experimental evidence for cyanamide guanidinylation of amines

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines two weaknesses: the questionable HCN→cyanamide photoisomerization and the carbon count error in ornithine synthesis. Weakest of the non-convergent pathways.
suggestions: Address both the cyanamide source and ornithine carbon count issues

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short but doubly flawed: HCN photoisomerization to cyanamide is uncertain, and ornithine branch has carbon count error.
suggestions: Needs fundamental corrections to both cyanamide and ornithine branches

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Convergent pathway using both HCN sources (hydrothermal and atmospheric) feeding into the compound 46 route with urea-derived cyanamide. Robustness is enhanced by dual HCN sourcing. All steps through the cyanosulfidic core are well-supported by Patel et al. Environmental progression from hydrothermal to surface to biochemical is clean. One of the strongest pathways.

Full convergent network incorporating all branches. The compound 46 branch is strong, but the ornithine branch has the carbon count error, and HCN photoisomerization to cyanamide is uncertain. As a complete network representation, it demonstrates excellent topology and convergence, but the ornithine sub-pathway is chemically flawed. The compound 46 sub-pathway within this network achieves the target successfully.
suggestions: The compound 46 sub-pathway within P10 is valid and should be the primary focus

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed synthesis network that faithfully implements the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic homologation pathway—the only experimentally detailed route to prebiotic arginine. The network excels in several areas: (1) excellent topology with proper hub molecules (HCN degree-6, NH₃ degree-6, cyanamide degree-4, β-aminopropionaldehyde degree-3); (2) strong environmental flow from hydrothermal (HCN/urea generation) through surface (cyanosulfidic photochemistry) to biochemical (hydrolysis); (3) faithful reproduction of the literature-supported compound 46 pathway; (4) clever use of dual HCN and dual cyanamide sources for robustness and pathway multiplicity. The primary weakness is the ornithine branch (pathways P5-P8), which contains a critical carbon count error: Strecker synthesis from 3-carbon β-aminopropionaldehyde + HCN produces a 4-carbon aminonitrile (2,4-diaminobutanenitrile), not 5-carbon ornithine α-aminonitrile. The molecular formula of mol_018 (C₄H₉N₃) is internally consistent with the Strecker product but inconsistent with ornithine (C₅). Additionally, the ornithine guanidinylation by cyanamide, while chemically plausible, has not been demonstrated prebiotically, as the literature explicitly notes. The HCN→cyanamide photoisomerization (rxn_007) is also speculative, though the urea route (rxn_005/rxn_006) provides a more credible alternative. Minor issues include the oversimplified serpentinization reaction (rxn_017) and the somewhat uncertain formate amidation (rxn_002). Despite these issues, pathways P1, P2, and P9 through the compound 46 route are chemically sound and well-supported by the literature. The network achieves the target through multiple valid routes. Rxn_018 (montmorillonite adsorption) is supported by recent literature on selective clay-arginine binding. Overall, this network is approved with the recommendation to correct the ornithine branch carbon count.
